front: Film: opens with a car accident killing composer Patrice de Courcy and his daughter; ends with a tear rolling down Julie's (Juliette Binoche) eye during a montage scored with Preisner's 'Song for the Unification of Europe'
Film: a Polish man smuggles himself in a suitcase from France back to Poland to seek revenge on his ex-wife (Julie Delpy), who divorced him for impotence

Film: Irene Jacob plays Valentine, a model in Geneva; an old judge spies via telephone on neighbors; ends with a storm wrecking a ferry on the English Channel and the final shot replicating Valentine's chewing gum poster
Film: the last film in the trilogy to feature Van den Budenmayer, a fictional Dutch composer invented by Kieślowski and Preisner; a montage dollying along phone lines opens it
Film: Irene Jacob plays both a Polish soprano and a French music teacher, both named Veronique, whose lives run parallel; a green-tinged sequence shows the teacher watching a marionette show
back: The Double Life of Véronique (Krzysztof Kieślowski)

front: Film: a TV miniseries loosely inspired by the Ten Commandments, set in a Warsaw housing complex; an unnamed witness (Artur Barcis) appears in nearly every episode; two episodes were expanded into A Short Film About Killing and A Short Film About Love

Film: first episode — a boy drowns in a skating accident despite computer calculations showing the ice was thick enough; second episode — a dying man is revived when a bee crawls out of a glass of strawberry syrup

Director: the Three Colours trilogy is themed on the colors of the French flag (liberty, equality, fraternity); his regular composer Zbigniew Preisner co-created the fictional Dutch composer Van den Budenmayer
Director: died while writing a trilogy of scripts inspired by Dante's Divine Comedy
